attributes.stem:
A 35-year old man presents to his General Practitioner with a two-day history of hearing loss in his right ear. He has no tinnitus, vertigo, nausea, ear pain or discharge. He has a medical history of type II diabetes and his only regular medication is metformin. He is a non-smoker and drinks about two glasses of wine most days.

On examination, his blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg and pulse rate is 90/min. Both tympanic membranes appear healthy and mobile, with no evidence of a middle ear effusion. His BMI is 32 kg/m2. Tuning fork tests are performed - the Weber test lateralises to his left ear; his Rinne is positive bilaterally. A high-quality audiogram in a sound proof booth shows asymmetrical sensorineural hearing loss on the right.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?
options: A short course of high dose oral corticosteroids
Full blood examination, blood glucose and HbA1C levels
attributes.explanation:
It is important to have an understanding of the underlying anatomy when considering hearing loss as it makes understanding the interpretation of the two common tests we use, the Weber Test and Rinne Test, easier to understand.

Broadly speaking, in hearing loss we consider two main systems that are responsible for allowing us to perceive sounds; these are the sound-conducting system and the sound-transducing system.

  • Sound-conducting system: This is composed of the outer ear (pinna, external auditory canal) and the middle ear (ossicles, tympanic membrane). Vibration is the main manner in which these signals are transmitted.

  • Sound-transducing system: This is composed of the cochlear, the auditory nerve and central processing centres. Vibration signals are transmitted as nerve impulses, and processed so we can appreciate them as sound.

It is important to note that the inner ear can process vibration both via the air conduction pathway (ie. the air conducting system), but also directly by bone conduction due to the anatomical relations in the ear. However, the inner ear is significantly more sensitive to sound via air conduction than bone conduction.

There are two main areas in which problems can arise - the conducting system or the transducing system (or, in some situations, both). A problem in the conducting system is referred to as conductive hearing loss, whereas a problem in the transducing system is referred to as sensorineural hearing loss.

  • Some causes of conductive hearing loss: impacted wax, cholesteatoma or other foreign body, otitis externa or otitis media, perforated tympanic membrane.

  • Some causes of sensorineural hearing loss: labyrinthitis, Meniere's disease, tumour.

The Weber Test: this test involves the placement of a vibrating tuning fork (ideally 512 Hz) on the vertex of the head. The patient is then asked where they hear the sound the louder (eg. right side, left side, or the middle/no difference).

  • The sound being heard louder on a single side is referred to as lateralisation.

    • If the test lateralises to the affected ear (ie. hearing loss is reported in right ear, Weber test demonstrates lateralisation to the right) then we would be suspicious of conductive hearing loss in the affected ear (ie. right ear)

    • If the test lateralises to the unaffected ear (ie. hearing loss is reported in right ear, Weber test demonstrates lateralisation to the left) then we would be suspicious of sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear (ie. right ear)

    • No lateralisation can mean hearing is normal, or that symmetrical hearing loss is present.

  • The Weber test should not be performed in isolation. It is important to always perform the Rinne test alongside the Weber test to properly screen for the likely cause of a hearing deficit.

The Rinne Test: This test involves the placement of a vibrating tuning fork (ideally 512 Hz) on the patient's mastoid bone. Ask the patient to tell you when they can no longer hear the sound. At this time, move the tuning fork adjacent to the ear canal. Ask the patient to tell you when they can no longer hear the sound.

  • This assesses whether air conduction or bone conduction is working better in each ear.

    • A positive finding means that air conduction is better than bone conduction (ie. the tuning fork in front of the ear was still heard once it was moved from the mastoid)

    • An negative finding means that bone conduction is better than air conduction (ie. the tuning fork in front of the ear was not heard once it was moved from the mastoid). This means that sound vibrations are not being transmitted through the sound-conducting system (external and middle ear)

  • The Rinne test should not be performed in isolation. It is important to always perform the Weber test alongside the Rinne test to properly screen for the likely cause of a hearing deficit.

So in this case, with hearing loss in the right ear, a Weber test that lateralises to the left ear and Rinne positive bilaterally, there is evidence of sensorineural hearing loss of the right ear with no evidence of conductive hearing loss.

A short course of high dose oral corticosteroids is the most appropriate response. There is strong evidence of the benefit of steroids in the management of idiopathic sensorineural hearing loss, which accounts for the majoirty of cases of sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL).

Seeking an MRI brain and cerebro-pontine angles will be an important step in the management of the patient, however, it is not the most appropriate next step. An MRI is indicated at some stage to exclude an acoustic neuroma, but this is not urgent as these tumours are rare and slow-growing.

Hearing aid and check audiogram may be appropriate for this patient in the future, however, not for immediate management. The need for hearing aid and audiogram will be dependent on the underlying cause of the hearing loss and degree of recovery that the patient has.

Full blood examination, blood glucose and HbA1C levels are important factors to monitor in a diabetic patient, however, they will likely not impact our immediate management. Thus, this is not the most appropriate response. Routine blood testing is generally not recommended in the investigation of hearing loss.

Middle ear ventilating tube (grommet) are not appropriate for this patient. Grommets are typically indicated in patients with recurrent otitis media or otitis media with effusion. These are both causes of conductive hearing loss, whereas our patient has sensorineural hearing loss.
learningPoints: A short course of high dose oral corticosteroids is the most appropriate management for sudden-onset sensorineural hearing loss
references: Ahmadzai N, Kilty S, Cheng W, Esmaeilisaraji L, Wolfe D, Bonaparte JP, et al. A systematic review and network meta-analysis of existing pharmacologic therapies in patients with idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss. PLoS One. 2019 Sep 9;14(9):e0221713. doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0221713. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0221713

Foden N, Mehta N, Joseph T. Sudden onset hearing loss: causes, investigations and management. Aust Fam Physician. 2013 Sep;42(9):641–644. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/september/sudden-onset-hearing-loss/
Fishman JM, Cullen L. Investigating sudden hearing loss in adults. BMJ. 2018 Nov 12;363:k4273. doi: 10.1136/bmj.k4273. Available at: https://www.bmj.com/content/363/bmj.k4273
